coredata.dc:title: Mesoporous carbon/N-doped graphene quantum dots integrated molecularly imprinted polymer explored as smartphone-assisted optosensing device for terbuthylazine detection

coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent sensor based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and mesoporous carbon (CMK-3) incorporated into a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was developed for the determination of terbuthylazine. The determination was correlated with the diminishing fluorescence (FL) response of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe, caused by the binding between terbuthylazine and the imprinted cavities. The CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe displayed a linear response towards terbuthylazine from 0.20 to 100.0 μg L−1 with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.06 μg L−1. The quantitative analysis by the smartphone-assisted optosensing device was also performed by observing the color change of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe and converting the FL images to RGB values. The linear response of the portable optosensing device was achieved in the range of 5.0–50.0 μg L−1 and the LOD was 1.5 μg L−1. Fluorescent-based optosensor and smartphone-assisted optosensing device were applied to determine terbuthylazine in fruit juice, satisfactory recoveries were acquired in the range of 91.5–96.2 % and 88.4–97.2 %, respectively. The results obtained using the developed methods corresponded favorably to a HPLC. The distinctive features of the developed method included high sensitivity, good selectivity, rapid, convenient detection and cost-effectiveness.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fabrication procedure of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe for terbuthylazine detection (A) and the detection procedure of terbuthylazine by the portable optosensing device with the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe (B).

The FL responses of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe before (a) and after (b) terbuthylazine removal and the non-imprinted CMK-3@NIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe (c) (A) and the sensitivity of various optosensing nanoprobes for terbuthylazine detection (B).

TEM images of N-GQDs (A) with their particle size distribution histogram (B) and CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe (C). SEM images of CMK-3 (D) and CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe (E) with their particle size distribution histogram (F).
The effect of binding time between the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes and terbuthylazine (A), amount of CMK-3 (B), ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker (C) and pH of dispersion solution (D).

The FL spectra of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs (A) and CMK-3@NIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes (B) in the presence of different concentrations of terbuthylazine. The calibration curves are of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs (C) and CMK-3@NIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes (D) for terbuthylazine, and the photograph displays the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes in the absence and presence of various terbuthylazine concentrations (E).

The linear calibration curve of the smartphone-assisted optical device for the determination of terbuthylazine (A) and the comparison of linear responses obtained from the developed fluorescent-based optosensor and smartphone-assisted optical device against terbuthylazine concentrations (B), the selectivity of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes (C) and competitive binding study for terbuthylazine detection in the presence of different ratios of atrazine to terbuthylazine (D).

The linear correlation curves were produced between fluorescent-based optosensor and smartphone-assisted optical device (A), fluorescent-based optosensor and HPLC (B), and smartphone-assisted optical device and HPLC (C) for the determination of terbuthylazine, three replicates were determined for each concentration (n = 3). The HPLC chromatograms are of terbuthylazine in spiked orange juice sample (D) at 0.0 (a), 0.05 (b), 0.10 (c), 0.25 (d) and 0.50 (e) mg L−1.
The determination of terbuthylazine in real samples using the developed fluorescent-based optosensor and smartphone-assisted optical device based on the nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent sensor based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and mesoporous carbon (CMK-3) incorporated into a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was developed for the determination of terbuthylazine. The determination was correlated with the diminishing fluorescence (FL) response of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe, caused by the binding between terbuthylazine and the imprinted cavities. The CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe displayed a linear response towards terbuthylazine from 0.20 to 100.0 μg L−1 with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.06 μg L−1. The quantitative analysis by the smartphone-assisted optosensing device was also performed by observing the color change of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe and converting the FL images to RGB values. The linear response of the portable optosensing device was achieved in the range of 5.0–50.0 μg L−1 and the LOD was 1.5 μg L−1. Fluorescent-based optosensor and smartphone-assisted optosensing device were applied to determine terbuthylazine in fruit juice, satisfactory recoveries were acquired in the range of 91.5–96.2 % and 88.4–97.2 %, respectively. The results obtained using the developed methods corresponded favorably to a HPLC. The distinctive features of the developed method included high sensitivity, good selectivity, rapid, convenient detection and cost-effectiveness.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In agriculture applications, triazine herbicides have been extensively employed to eradicate various weeds and enhance crop yields (Kunene and Mahlambi, 2019). Terbuthylazine, an example of a triazine herbicide, has been utilized worldwide for many years due to its cost-effectiveness and high efficacy (Li et al., 2022). Nevertheless, the accumulation of residual terbuthylazine can lead to environmental pollution and contaminate agricultural products (Jing et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2014). As a result of the contamination of agricultural products by terbuthylazine, these residues can be transmitted to people through the food chain, potentially resulting in adverse effects such as eye and skin irritation, endocrine disruption, birth defects, hormone disorders and increased risks of cancer (Peng et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2021). To assure food safety, the maximum residue limit (MRL) of terbuthylazine has been implemented by the European Union (EU) at 0.01 mg kg−1 in vegetables and fruits. Thus, the development of an efficient and reliable method to determine terbuthylazine in agricultural products is required.

A variety of analytical methods for determining terbuthylazine have included high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Jing et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2018), gas chromatography (GC) (Mourão et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2021), electrochemical methods (Oriol et al., 2021) and colorimetric sensors (Chen et al., 2018). HPLC is the most commonly utilized technique to determine terbuthylazine. Even though this technique possesses desirable properties such as good separation, accuracy and precision, it has some limitations including long analysis times, the need for large volumes of organic solvents and use of costly instrumentation (Chullasat et al., 2017). These requirements also make its use in on-site analysis problematical. In addition to the mentioned methods, FL spectroscopy is an attractive alternative technique for the determination of single and dual target compounds owing to its short measurement time, straightforward analysis procedure and cost-effectiveness (Feng et al., 2019; Orachorn and Bunkoed, 2021). Furthermore, smartphone-integrated optosensing devices have been increasingly favored to determine target compounds because of their portability, rapid real-time detection and uncomplicated procedures (Hu et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023).

The enhancement of the sensitivity of the fluorescent sensor can be accomplished by employing sensing materials, particularly quantum dot (QD) nanoparticles. The unique optical characteristics of QDs demonstrate strong FL intensity, symmetric and narrow spectra and good photostability (Wu et al., 2018). Among a variety of QD types, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are an interesting family of potential sensing materials due to their low toxicity within the environment, high FL efficiency, good stability and ease of synthesis (Mehrzad-Samarin et al., 2017). To further increase the quantum yield of GQDs, doping GQDs with nitrogen proves to be an effective approach (Kashani et al., 2017). Recently, the use of N-GQDs as sensing materials has been reported for the fabrication of novel fluorescent probes to determine various compounds such as prometryn (Chumkaeo et al., 2024), florfenicol (Seangyai et al., 2023), sparfloxacin (Chansud et al., 2023), thiacloprid (Liu et al., 2018) and tetracycline (Zhang et al., 2020). The inherent lack of selectivity of the optical probe can be overcome by firming composites of QDs with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) (Gui and Jin, 2019). MIPs are highly cross-linked polymers containing specific imprinted sites within the polymer structure which are tailored in shape, functional groups and size for target compounds (Arabi et al., 2020). Numerous beneficial properties of MIPs consist of high specificity, good stability, cost-effectiveness and straightforward synthesis (Fu, et al., 2019; Fang et al., 2021). The adsorption interactions between the fluorescent probe and terbuthylazine can be also improved by compositing the MIP-N-GQDs probe with high affinity materials. Mesoporous carbon (CMK-3) is one type of mesoporous carbon which has acquired significant attention for its utilization as an adsorption material. The notable properties of CMK-3 include a large surface area, high thermal and chemical stability, high pore volume and low-cost (Liu et al., 2022). Because of its structure, CMK-3 is capable of adsorbing terbuthylazine by strong hydrophobic and π-π interactions and hydrogen bonding.

In the present work, a novel nanocomposite fluorescent probe was designed and fabricated by combining the CMK-3 and N-GQDs into MIP material. N-GQDs was utilized to improve the sensitivity of the sensing probe, CMK-3 was used to enhance the affinity binding between the target analyte and sensing probe, it has a high potential to enhance the adsorption of terbuthylazine through π-π and hydrophobic interactions as well as hydrogen bonding. While MIP was utilized to improve the selectivity of the sensing probe which can be applied to determine analyte in complex samples. To facilitate on-site determination, a portable optosensing device based on a composite nanoprobe was developed using a ColorPicker application on a smartphone to assess the FL images of samples. The ability of the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe was evaluated by applying it to determine terbuthylazine levels in grapes, oranges, pomegranates and carrots. The accuracy of a nanoprobe-based optosensing device was verified through a comparison of its performance with that of a HPLC method.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris-HMA) and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). Terbuthylazine, atrazine, simazine, propazine, simetryn, terbumeton and trietazine were obtained from Dr. Ehrenstorfer (Germany). Citric acid 1-hydrate was purchased from KemAus (Australia). Mesoporous carbon (CMK-3) was produced by XFNANO (China). Sodium hydroxide was obtained from Loba Chemie (India). Di-sodium hydrogen orthophosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O) and sodium dihydrogen orthophosphate (NaH2PO4·2H2O) were supplied by Ajax Finechem (Australia). Ammonia and ethanol were supplied by RCI Labscan (Thailand).

A Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to measure FL intensity (Agilent, California, United States). A UV-2600i UV-Vis spectrophotometer was used to acquire UV-Vis absorption spectra (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). A 1100 series Hewlett-Packard HPLC system coupled with a diode array detector (DAD) was used to determine terbuthylazine (Agilent Technologies, Waldbronn, Germany). A BX Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscope (PerkinElmer, Massachusetts, USA) was used to examine the functional groups of the nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe and its related materials. A Talos F200i field emission transmission electron microscope (FETEM) (Thermo Scientific, Brno, Czech Republic) and an Apreo field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) (FEI, Eindhoven, Netherlands) were utilized to characterize the morphologies of the nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe and its associated materials. A ZetaPALS zeta potential analyzer (Brookhaven, New York, USA) was employed to assess the zeta potential of the nanocomposite probe. A D2 Phaser X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany) was utilized to acquire XRD patterns of the nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe and its component materials. The energy dispersive X-ray (EDS) spectra were produced with an Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (Oxford Instruments, England).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis procedure of N-GQDs was slightly altered from previous work (Lin et al., 2015). Initially, 0.50 g of tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane and 2.00 g of citric acid were put into a round bottom flask, thoroughly blended and sealed with aluminum foil. Next, the mixture was heated at 225°C on a heating mantle for 12 min. The orange solid precipitate of N-GQDs was acquired and subsequently cooled to room temperature. Next, the produced N-GQDs were dispersed in 5.0 mL of deionized water and adjusted to pH 7.0 by adding 0.25 M NaOH and stored at 4 °C until used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of the nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe was achieved by a facile sol-gel method as demonstrated in Fig. 1A. First, 3.40 mg of terbuthylazine (template molecule), 53 μL of APTES (monomer) and 5.0 mL of ethanol were mixed in a dark bottle. After stirring for 1 h, 5.0 mL of the prepared N-GQDs were added into the mixture solution and stirred for 30 min. Then, 2.00 mg of CMK-3 were added and stirred for 30 min. To generate the imprinted polymer, 300 μL of TEOS (cross-linker) and 150 μL of NH3 (25 % w/v) were added and the entire solution was continuously stirred for 4 h. In the template removal step, the polymer matrix was repeatedly washed with ethanol under sonication (three times) to create specific imprinted sites for terbuthylazine. Finally, the synthesized CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe was collected by centrifugation and subsequently stored in a desiccator. Under identical conditions, the non-imprinted polymer (NIP) of CMK-3@NIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe was also synthesized, but without the addition of terbuthylazine (template molecule).

The FL emission intensity of the nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs or CMK-3@NIP-N-GQDs probes was measured using a spectrofluorometer at 25 °C. Firstly, 10.0 mL of phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.0) was utilized to disperse 10.0 mg of the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe. Next, 100 μL of either terbuthylazine standard or sample solution were thoroughly mixed with 300 μL of the dispersed CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs solution. The complete binding between terbuthylazine and the nanocomposite probe was achieved by incubating the mixture solution for 20 min. In the final step, 200 μL of the obtained solution were analyzed by setting an excitation wavelength of 328 nm and recording an emission wavelength ranging from 350 to 600 nm with a slit width of 10 nm.

To obtain the highest sensitivity with shortest analysis time. The fabrication and determination were optimized including binding time, amount of CMK-3, ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker, pH of dispersion solution and washing solvent. The sensitivity is the slope of calibration curve plotting between the concentration of terbuthylazine (x-axis) and the ratio of fluorescence intensity in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of terbuthylazine (y-axis).

The portable optosensing device was created using a designed ultraviolet black box (22 × 27 × 14.5 cm) coupled with a smartphone (Hu et al., 2023). In the fabrication procedure of the optical device, a LED light source (365 nm, 3 W) was employed and connected with a power bank to produce the FL response of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe. In addition, the sample cell holder was appropriately positioned and the filter (400 nm) was fixed between a sample cell and smartphone to reduce interference from other excitation beams during the visual on-site detection process. The distance between the sample cell and smartphone was 3.5 cm.

The detection procedure of terbuthylazine by the designed portable optosensing device was performed as demonstrated in Fig. 1B. Firstly, the nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe was dispersed in pH 6.0 of phosphate buffer solution. Then, 400 μL of sample solution was fully mixed with 1.2 mL of the prepared CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs solution and incubated for 20 min before being transferred into a cuvette. The acquired mixture was placed in an ultraviolet black box. The FL images were recorded and analyzed using a ColorPicker application on a smartphone in terms of red (R), green (G) and blue (B) values.

Under UV light, the FL image of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes demonstrated a bright blue color. After binding of terbuthylazine by the fabricated nanoprobes, the bright blue colors of the nanoprobe images were changed to darker shades as the concentrations of terbuthylazine were enhanced. The grayscale value (I) was subsequently derived from the RGB value of the FL image using the Eq. 1 (Hu et al., 2023):
(1) I = 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114B

The developed smartphone-assisted optical device can be utilized for the quantitative analysis of terbuthylazine by evaluating the change of mean grayscale value (IM), as derived from the Eq. 2 (Xiao et al., 2020):
(2) IM = (IBlank - ISample)/(IBlank)

Where IBlank and ISample are the grayscale value of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes without and with terbuthylazine, respectively. The linear relationship of the developed smartphone assisted-optical device was established by plotting the capability range between the mean grayscale value (IM) and terbuthylazine concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment of grapes, oranges, pomegranates and carrots samples
ce:section.ce:para: Grape, orange, pomegranate and carrot juice were first prepared by centrifugation at 3075 g for 10 min and filtration through a nylon syringe filter (0.2 μm of pore size and 13 mm of diameter) to remove particles in samples. To eliminate some interferences in fruit juices such as vitamins, minerals, pigments, carbohydrates and acids, the obtained sample solution was diluted 100 times with deionized water before being analyzed by the developed optosensor and smartphone-assisted optosensing device using the nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe.

In actual sample applications, both the developed fluorescent-based optosensor and portable smartphone-assisted optical devices using the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes were utilized to detect terbuthylazine in real samples. To assess the accuracy of both the methods, juice samples were spiked with terbuthylazine standards at four different concentrations (5.0, 10.0, 25.0 and 50.0 μg L−1). The percentage of relative recovery (RR) was subsequently calculated for accuracy evaluation, derived from the Eq. 3 as follows:
(3) RR(%) = (CSpiked-CNon-spiked)/CAdded × 100
Where CAdded is the terbuthylazine concentration added to the actual sample, CSpiked and CNon-spiked are the terbuthylazine concentration in spiked and non-spiked actual samples, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of terbuthylazine by an HPLC method
ce:section.ce:para: Terbuthylazine was determined using an HPLC-DAD system with a Fortis C18 analytical column (5 μm of particle size, 15 cm of length × 4.6 mm of ID). The mobile phase used an isocratic mode of ultrapure water (35 %) and acetonitrile (65 %) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1. The quantitative analysis of terbuthylazine was performed by measuring the absorbance, which was detected using a diode array detector (DAD) at a wavelength of 220 nm. The sample injection volume was 20 μL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the sensing probe and related materials
ce:section.ce:para: The complete removal of the template from the nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe was confirmed by comparing the FL response of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe after terbuthylazine removal with that of the non-imprinted CMK-3@NIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe. The FL spectrum of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe demonstrated a notably lower intensity before the removal of the terbuthylazine template ( Fig. 2A(a)). After washing the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe with ethanol (Fig. 2A(b)), the intensity was enhanced to almost that of the non-imprinted CMK-3@NIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe (Fig. 2A(c)). This result confirmed the complete elution of terbuthylazine template from the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe.

To assess the ability of the fabricated nanoprobes for terbuthylazine detection, a variety of nanoprobes were tested, consisting of the NIP-N-GQDs, MIP-N-GQDs, CMK-3@NIP-N-GQDs and CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes. Considering the highest sensitivity, the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes demonstrated superior binding efficiency for terbuthylazine (Fig. 2B). This phenomenon was attributed to numerous adsorption sites of CMK-3 and specific imprinted cavities presented in the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes, leading to a high potential for terbuthylazine binding. Due to its structure, CMK-3 can interact with terbuthylazine through hydrophobic and π-π interactions and hydrogen bonding. In contrast, the NIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe displayed the lowest sensitivity toward terbuthylazine because there were no adsorption sites of CMK-3 and specific imprinted cavities for terbuthylazine binding. This result confirmed that the nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe had the greatest potential for the detection of terbuthylazine.

The identification of functional groups of the nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe and its components was performed using FTIR spectroscopy. The significant IR peaks of N-GQDs (Fig. S1a) appeared at 3415 cm−1 for N-H and O-H stretching, 2971 cm−1 for sp2 C-H stretching, 1643 cm−1 of CO stretching, 1578 cm−1 of CC stretching, 1400 cm−1 of C-H bending, 1273 cm−1 of C-O stretching and 1107 cm−1 of C-N stretching. The adsorption material of CMK-3 (Fig. S1b) had characteristic peaks of 3415, 2971, 1578, 1400 and 1273 cm−1 related to O-H stretching, sp2 C-H stretching, CC stretching, C-H bending and C-O stretching, respectively. The FTIR spectrum of terbuthylazine template (Fig. S1c) demonstrated unique peaks at 3261 cm−1 of N-H stretching, 2875 cm−1 of sp3 C-H stretching, 1550 cm−1 of CN stretching, 1400 and 1340 cm−1 of C-H bending, 1129 cm−1 of C-N stretching and 807 cm−1 of C-Cl stretching. Before terbuthylazine removal, the synthesized CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe (Fig. S1d) exhibited characteristic peaks of terbuthylazine and unique peaks indicating MIP formation, including 3415 cm−1 of O-H stretching, 1084 cm−1 of Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching and 786 and 463 cm−1 of Si-O vibration bands. The characteristic peaks of the nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe after terbuthylazine removal (Fig. S1e) were observed at the same wavenumber of the nanocomposite non-imprinted CMK-3@NIP-N-GQDs probe (Fig. S1f) with no presence of terbuthylazine peaks. The successful fabrication of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe was therefore verified through FTIR spectra.

The FL emission efficiency of the synthesized N-GQDs, MIP-N-GQDs and CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes can be evaluated in terms of quantum yield (QY). In the comparative method for QY measurement, quinine sulfate with a QY of 54 % is normally utilized as the FL standard (Lu et al., 2016). The QYs of N-GQDs, MIP-N-GQDs, and CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes were respectively assessed to be 64.2, 42.3, and 33.1 %, indicating that the synthesized N-GQDs and nanocomposite probes exhibited favorable optical characteristics for optosensor applications.

The N-GQDs displayed an identical spherical shape and varied in size from 3 to 7 nm ( Fig. 3A and B). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe is demonstrated in Fig. 3C. The fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe also revealed a spherical shape with a particle size distribution in the range of 450–750 nm. In the SEM images, the surface morphology of CMK-3 demonstrated a uniformly arranged worm-like structure (Fig. 3D), while the nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe exhibited a relatively rough surface attributed to imprinted cavities formed on the polymer layer for terbuthylazine (Fig. 3E). The particle size distribution histogram of the sensing probe is shown in Fig. 3F.

The zeta potentials of the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes before and after interaction with terbuthylazine were evaluated by dispersing the nanoprobes in phosphate buffer (pH 6.0) prior to the analysis process. The CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe had a negative charge value of zeta potential (–35.8 mV) related to various functional groups including amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe (Fig. S2). The negative charge of the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe indicated both the low aggregation possibility and high stability of the nanoprobe (Hoti et al., 2023). The zeta potential values of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe and terbuthylazine were negative and near neutral, respectively. After interaction with terbuthylazine, the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe exhibited a more negative charge (–39.3 mV), indicating that hydrogen bonding was a possible major interaction between the nanoprobe and terbuthylazine (Niu et al., 2017).

X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) was further employed to examine the structure of N-GQDs, CMK-3 and the nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe. The XRD pattern of N-GQDs revealed a broad peak at 2θ of 18.5° associated with the (002) plane of a graphitic structure (Fig. S3a). The CMK-3 has an amorphous structure, which can be confirmed by two broad diffraction peaks at 2θ of 22.7° and 44.0°, corresponding to (002) and (100) planes, respectively (Fig. S3b). The XRD pattern of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe demonstrated a broad characteristic peak at 2θ of 22.7° related to (002) plane (Fig. S3c). The peak intensity of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs was reduced because of the imprinted cavities within the polymer layer, resulting in a more amorphous structure (Yang et al., 2018).

The element composition of CMK-3 and CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe were further investigated using EDS analysis. The elemental mapping results of CMK-3 demonstrated a predominant C content (89.9 %) and minor O content (10.1 %) (Fig. S4a and S4b). As illustrated in Fig. S4c-f, the element composition of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe included C (54.8 %), N (3.8 %), O (34.7 %) and Si (6.7 %) with a uniform distribution. The main presence of C was related to both CMK-3 and N-GQDs within the nanoprobe. The presence of N was associated with N-GQDs and amino functional groups in the MIP layer. The minor content of O was due to oxygen-based functional groups from CMK-3 and N-GQDs. In addition, Si content appeared owing to the formation of the polymer layer of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe. The EDS results ensured the successful incorporation of CMK-3 and N-GQDs into the MIP, demonstrating a homogeneous chemical composition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for the fabrication of sensing probe and determination conditions

ce:section.ce:para: The effect of binding time is crucial to define a suitable time for the re-binding between specific imprinted cavities on the surface of the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe and terbuthylazine (Chullasat et al., 2018). The binding time was examined by measuring quenching efficiency (F0/F) every 2 min from 0 to 30 min. The quenching efficiency increased with binding time for up to 20 min and subsequently remained invariable ( Fig. 4A). As a result, the appropriate binding time with the greatest quenching efficiency was 20 min for the detection of terbuthylazine.

The adsorption sites of nanoprobe can be enhanced by incorporating CMK-3 into nanoprobe, thereby improving the binding affinity of the nanoprobe toward terbuthylazine. To create the nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe, different amounts of CMK-3 from 1.0 to 3.0 mg were utilized. The fabricated nanoprobes using 2.0 mg of CMK-3 provided the greatest sensitivity (Fig. 4B). A low quantity of CMK-3 resulted in reduced sensitivity owing to the inadequate number of adsorption sites on the surface of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes, which had limited binding ability for terbuthylazine. Meanwhile, low sensitivity was also observed with an excess quantity of CMK-3, possibly because the generated imprinted cavities were blocked, leading to reduced binding ability for terbuthylazine (Orachorn and Bunkoed, 2022). As a result, 2.0 mg of CMK-3 was optimal for the fabrication of CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of imprinted cavities on the surface of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe primarily relies on the quantity of terbuthylazine template, monomer and cross-linker (Hasanah et al., 2021). Different mole ratios of terbuthylazine template to APTES to TEOS were examined to produce a strong and efficient nanoprobe for the binding of terbuthylazine. During nanoprobe fabrication, APTES can form strong interactions with terbuthylazine template through hydrogen bonding before polymerization, leading to the subsequent creation of specific imprinted cavities for terbuthylazine. Next, TEOS was utilized to fix the terbuthylazine-APTES complexes and generate a strong three-dimensional MIP nanoprobe. Considering the greatest sensitivity, the suitable molar proportion of terbuthylazine to APTES to TEOS was determined to be 1:15:90 for the fabrication of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes (Fig. 4C). A limited quantity of APTES offered decreased sensitivity because monomer levels were insufficient to interact effectively around terbuthylazine template, resulting in a low number of imprinted cavities produced for the binding of terbuthylazine. A high quantity of APTES also led to reduced sensitivity because excess monomer can create non-imprinted sites, which can impede the binding ability of nanoprobe toward terbuthylazine. Similarly, low sensitivity was observed when both low and high quantities of TEOS were utilized for the fabrication of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes. When insufficient TEOS was used, low sensitivity occurred owing to the lack of rigidity and the instability of the generated polymer layer of the nanoprobes. In contrast, too high a quantity of TEOS resulted in decreased sensitivity since an excessive amount of cross-linker can create a dense (highly rigid) polymer structure, potentially obstructing the formation of imprinted cavities for terbuthylazine binding. As a result, the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes were most efficiently fabricated using an appropriate ratio of 1:15:90.

In the detection procedure, the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes were prepared in the dispersion solution with an appropriate pH value before binding with terbuthylazine to control the surrounding environment. To achieve the greatest binding efficiency between the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes and terbuthylazine, various pH values of dispersion solution were tested at pH 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0 and 8.0. Considering the best sensitivity, the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes dispersed in phosphate buffer at pH 6.0 demonstrated the most appropriate condition for terbuthylazine binding (Fig. 4D). The decreased sensitivity was acquired at pH values lower and higher than 6.0. The reduced sensitivity observed at low pH can be attributed to the protonation of amino groups presented on both terbuthylazine and the surface of the fabricated nanoprobes. This phenomenon diminished the potential for hydrogen bonding and induced electrostatic repulsion between terbuthylazine and the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe. In contrast, sensitivity was also decreased at high pH due to the ionization of the silica structure of the nanoprobes and deprotonation of terbuthylazine (Amiri et al., 2021), resulting in low binding ability of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe for terbuthylazine. Therefore, the effective detection of terbuthylazine was obtained by dispersing the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes in phosphate buffer at pH 6.0.

After the polymer layer of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes was formed, a variety of solvents were considered for the removal of the terbuthylazine template. In this work, methanol, ethanol, acetonitrile, acetone and hexane were examined as washing solvents. Among the tested solvents, ethanol exhibited the greatest effective feature for the complete removal of the terbuthylazine template from the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes within three cycles. Additionally, the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes using ethanol as washing solvent offered the highest sensitivity for terbuthylazine detection (Fig. S5). Therefore, ethanol was selected as the most suitable washing solvent for the removal of template.

To verify the performance of the developed optosensor, the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe was tested under optimal conditions for the detection of terbuthylazine at varying concentrations. The significant quenching of the FL response of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes occurred with increasing terbuthylazine concentrations ( Fig. 5A). On the other hand, there was a slight decrease in the FL response of the non-imprinted CMK-3@NIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes (Fig. 5B). In the optosensor, the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes demonstrated an excellent linear concentration range of terbuthylazine from 0.20 to 100.0 μg L−1 (Fig. 5C) and the calibration curve of the CMK-3@NIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe for terbuthylazine is illustrated in Fig. 5D. The photograph of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes with different concentrations of terbuthylazine from 0.0 to 100.0 μg L−1 was performed under UV light, as displayed in Fig. 5E. To determine the minimum detectable concentration of terbuthylazine by an optosensor with the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.06 μg L−1, derived from 3 SDBlank/slope. Meanwhile, the minimum detectable concentration of terbuthylazine can be quantitatively assessed as the limit of quantification (LOQ) to be 0.20 μg L−1, calculated from 10 SDBlank/slope. The low LOD and LOQ indicated that ultra-trace concentrations of terbuthylazine can be detected by the developed optosensor using the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes.

The quantitative analysis of terbuthylazine by the developed optosensor was carried out by measuring the change of the intensity quenching of the FL response. The Stern-Volmer equation was utilized to define the efficiency of FL quenching for the determination of terbuthylazine as follows, Eq. 4. (Kim and Chang, 2016):
(4) F0/F= 1 + K SV[CTB]

Where F and F0 are the intensity of the FL response of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes with and without terbuthylazine, respectively. [CTB] is the terbuthylazine concentration and KSV is the quenching efficiency constant. According to the equation, the linear relationship was assessed by plotting the ability range (ability range refers to the acceptable linear range of the developed method, considering a coefficient of determination (R²) greater than 0.99) between the quenching efficiency (F0/F) and terbuthylazine concentration [CTB]. Additionally, KSV represents the sensitivity of the developed optosensor employing the fabricated nanoprobes for terbuthylazine quantification. To evaluate the specificity of the imprinted cavities produced on the surface of the nanoprobe, specificity can be described in terms of the imprinting factor (IF). The sensitivity of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe (KSV(MIP)) was compared with that of the non-imprinted CMK-3@NIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe (KSV(NIP)), which was calculated to be 3.4 as an IF value of the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes for terbuthylazine detection. Based on its high IF value, the imprinted cavities of the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe demonstrated outstanding specificity, indicating high capability for detecting terbuthylazine.

In the detection procedure, the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe contained amino groups (-NH2) on its surface, which could potentially form hydrogen bonds with terbuthylazine. The phenomenon of FL quenching was subsequently observed, possibly attributed to the transfer of electrons from the conduction band of the N-GQDs in the fabricated nanoprobes to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of terbuthylazine. The mechanism of the FL quenching can be further ensured by considering the FL emission spectrum of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe and the UV-Vis spectrum of terbuthylazine. As demonstrated in Fig. S6, the regions of both spectra were obviously separated to confirm FL quenching caused by the electron transfer process. The results implied that the quenching of the probe by terbuthylazine could be explained by dynamic quenching (Fu et al., 2017).

A satisfactory linear response was achieved in the concentration range of 5.0–50.0 μg L−1 ( Fig. 6A). The LOD (3 SDBlank/slope) and LOQ (10 SDBlank/slope) for terbuthylazine were 1.5 and 5.0 μg L−1, respectively. The developed optosensing method provided a lower LOD of terbuthylazine than MRLs defined by the EU, indicating high sensitivity for the detection of terbuthylazine. Additionally, the linear response of the designed portable optosensing device was similar to that of the developed fluorescent-based optosensor in its ability to determine terbuthylazine in the concentration range of 5.0–50.0 μg L−1 (Fig. 6B). These results affirmed that the smartphone-assisted optical device using the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe exhibited high potential for the visual on-site detection of terbuthylazine with good accuracy.

The binding ability of the imprinted cavities produced on the surface of CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes was investigated by determining terbuthylazine along with its analog structures including atrazine, simazine, propazine, simetryn, terbumeton and trietazine. The greatest sensitivity of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes was achieved for the determination of terbuthylazine (Fig. 6C). In contrast, the non-imprinted CMK-3@NIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes displayed significantly lower sensitivity to terbuthylazine and other triazine herbicides owing to the absence of imprinted cavities on the surface of the nanoprobes (Li et al., 2021).

The selectivity of the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes was further confirmed through a competitive binding study. The fabricated nanoprobes were employed to determine terbuthylazine in the presence of a variety of atrazine concentrations. The sensitivity of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes demonstrated no noticeable difference in the absence and presence of atrazine (Fig. 6D). These results ensured that the imprinted cavities created on the polymer layer of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes exhibited high specificity for terbuthylazine.

To evaluate the stability of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes in the dispersion solution at pH 6.0, the FL response of the fabricated nanoprobes was monitored over a period ranging from 0 to 420 min. The FL emission was unchanged over a duration time of 420 min with an acceptable relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.42 % (Fig. S7A), which was a sufficient period for the determination of terbuthylazine. Additionally, after a prolonged storage period of over 6 months in the auto desiccator at ambient temperature (27–29 °C), the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes still exhibited an identical FL response compared to a new batch of nanoprobes. The results confirmed that the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes had good stability under detecting conditions (Sa-nguanprang et al., 2023).

To further examine the reproducibility of the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes, the fabrication of six different batches of nanoprobes was performed under identical experiment conditions. Six batches of the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes demonstrated an acceptable RSD of 1.74 % (Fig. S7B) towards the determination of terbuthylazine. As a result, the fabrication procedure of the nanoprobe displayed great reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of terbuthylazine in real samples by fluorescent-based optosensor and smartphone-assisted optical devices
ce:section.ce:para: The relative recoveries of terbuthylazine acquired from the developed fluorescent-based optosensor were acceptable according to AOAC guidelines (AOAC, 2016), ranging from 91.5% to 96.2 %. Satisfactory RSDs values below 6 % were also achieved ( Table 1). Meanwhile, the accuracy of the smartphone-assisted optical device was also satisfactory with relative recoveries of terbuthylazine in the range of 88.4–97.2 % and RSDs lower than 5 %. To evaluate the analytical relationship in actual sample applications, the linear correlation curve was plotted by comparing terbuthylazine concentrations found in spiked juice samples obtained from the fluorescent-based optosensor and smartphone-assisted optical method. The analytical results of the developed fluorescent-based optosensor were consistent with those of the smartphone-assisted optical devices, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9960 ( Fig. 7A). Furthermore, the analytical results of both the fluorescent-based optosensor and smartphone-assisted optical device based on the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes also corresponded to those of the HPLC method, with correlation coefficients of 0.9978 and 0.9977, respectively (Fig. 7B and C). The HPLC chromatograms of terbuthylazine spiked in juice samples at different concentrations are displayed in Fig. 7D. These results insisted that the designed portable smartphone-assisted optical device with the fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe can serve as a potential and reliable alternative method for the on-site determination of terbuthylazine in juice samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the developed optosensing methods with other previous works
ce:section.ce:para: The performances of the developed fluorescent-based optosensor and smartphone-assisted optical device using the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes were compared with those of other previous published works for the quantitative analysis of terbuthylazine ( Table 2). The developed optosensing methods provided wide linear response ranges and low LODs with satisfactory levels of accuracy and precision. The sensitivity of both developed fluorescent-based optosensor and portable optical device were greater than or comparable to those of various previous works. The reliability of the developed method in term of recovery is better than (Li et al., 2022; Mourão et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2021) or comparable with other previous reports (Jing et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2018; Rodríguez-González et al., 2016). Additionally, the developed smartphone-assisted optical device exhibited some superior features compared to other works, including uncomplicated measurement, portability, real-time on-site detection, low solvent usage and inexpensive analysis cost. While, most of the previous works are chromatographic techniques which required expert operation, long analysis time, expensive instruments, and complicated sample preparation procedure is needed (Kunene and Mahlambi, 2019; Wang et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2014). The fabricated CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobes contained highly selective imprinted cavities tailored for binding terbuthylazine, which can be beneficial to both determine target analyte levels and minimize various interferences in real samples. Therefore, the developed portable optosensing device based on the CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs nanoprobe can be utilized as an effective method with distinctive properties for the on-site determination of trace terbuthylazine in complex samples.

A unique optosensing nanoprobe for terbuthylazine detection was successfully designed and fabricated using CMK-3 and N-GQDs integrated into MIP. The fabricated nanoprobes had various outstanding advantages including high selectivity, good sensitivity, a large number of adsorption sites and ease of preparation. The nanocomposite CMK-3@MIP-N-GQDs probe was effectively utilized with both a fluorescent-based optosensor and a smartphone-assisted optical device to determine trace levels of terbuthylazine in fruit juice samples. Both the developed optosensing methods exhibited good accuracy with recoveries ranging from 88.4 % to 97.2 % and precision with RSDs lower than 6 %. Low detection limits of terbuthylazine were achieved at 0.06 and 1.5 μg L−1 for the fluorescent-based optosensor and the portable optosensing device, respectively. Furthermore, the designed smartphone-assisted optical platform demonstrated great potential for the real-time on-site detection of terbuthylazine, with numerous benefits such as convenient portability, uncomplicated process, outstanding selectivity and good sensitivity. The developed optosensing strategy can be adapted and utilized for the future determination of other target compounds in a variety of complex samples. One limitation of the proposed method is suitable for the determination of single target analyte, however, if there are other compounds need to be detected, the modification of the proposed method can be performed for the determination of the target compounds. One limitation of the proposed method which is suitable for the determination of single target analyte, however, if there are other compounds need to be detected, the modification of the proposed method can be performed for the determination of the target compounds.
